narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared a strong profile with a high family income bracket, a solid LSAT score of 37—right above the typical student's 36.7—and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of applicants. Your undergraduate GPA sits at 3.10, a bit below the average of 3.21, while your Year 1 school decile ranking placed you at the top in the 1st decile, far exceeding the average 6th decile position. However, your Year 3 decile ranking dropped to the 2nd, compared to the usual 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 48% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile; that drop signaled to the model a potential late-stage struggle not seen in most successful bar passers. Nearly balancing it out was your undergraduate GPA, which pulled strongly in the opposite direction toward predicting success, showing the model viewed your college record as a key strength. Your LSAT score also worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing by demonstrating the analytical skills typical of those who succeed. Overall, while your early strengths and test performance stood out positively against averages, the Year 3 dip was the most influential concern, tipping the scales despite your otherwise competitive Hispanic male profile in a field where persistence matters. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building Year 3-equivalent experience through targeted prep programs to boost that decile ranking, then reapply with updated credentials showing upward momentum. Law schools value growth stories like yours, and with focused effort, you can shift that probability in your favor next time.
